definition: A two-dimensional parameter space where core inflation of 2.0-3.5% combined with fiscal deficits of 4.5-7.0% GDP produces structurally unstable stock-bond correlation. Below the corridor on either variable, one force dominates and correlation resolves. The US currently occupies this corridor (core PCE ~2.7%, deficit ~6.5% GDP).
evidence: Analytically derived from 4 G10 episodes occupying this parameter space simultaneously
Below inflation corridor: negative correlation reasserts; above 4% sustained: positive locks in
Below deficit corridor (<4% declining): negative correlation reasserts
pair_0 judged this 'more operationally useful' than unidimensional inflation focus
Parameter boundaries carry ±1pp uncertainty; concept more robust than specific boundaries
Below corridor on either variable produces stable correlation regime
US occupies corridor: core PCE ~2.7%, deficit ~6.5% GDP
Parameter boundaries carry ±1pp uncertainty from small sample
open_questions: Are the parameter boundaries robust across different institutional contexts?
Is the corridor concept applicable outside G10 economies?
What event would push the US out of the corridor on either dimension?
Does the corridor shift over time with structural changes in the economy?
Are the specific boundary parameters (2.0-3.5% inflation, 4.5-7.0% deficit) robust?
How many additional G10 episodes would be needed to validate the boundaries?
Can the corridor framework be extended to non-G10 economies?
